coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent near-infrared emitting carbon dots derived from lemon juice and its bioimaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Hui
Zhou, Xuanxuan
Qin, Botao
Zhou, Ziyuan
Zhao, Yunpeng
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained intensive interests in the recent years due to their unique optical properties and widespread applications. However, the facile synthesis of highly efficient near-infrared emitting CDs (NIR-CDs) from renewable and low-cost natural resources is still a challenge to be addressed for their further applications, particularly in the bioimaging fields. Herein, NIR-CDs with a high quantum yield of 31% in water were synthesized by heating a formamide solution of pulp-free lemon juice. The as-obtained NIR-CDs possess an average size of 5.7 nm and a nitrogen content of approximately 20%, and exhibit an excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission at 704 nm. Further studies suggest that the intense near-infrared emission of NIR-CDs is originated from their surface states, and that the energy gaps of these surface states are modulated by both the particle size and the nitrogen-doping amount. In addition, characterized with good photostability and low cytotoxicity, these NIR-CDs can be used as an excellent fluorescent probe for bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass
Near-infrared emission
Surface state
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the NIR-CDs. (B) AFM image of the NIR-CDs (inset: height profile along the line in (B)).
(A) Raman spectrum and (B) FTIR spectrum of the NIR-CDs.
(A) XPS spectra of the obtained NIR-CDs. (B–D) High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s, N1s and O1s of the NIR -CDs, respectively.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectrum and PL emission spectra of the NIR-CDs excited by different wavelengths of light. The inset shows the photographs of the NIR-CDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and irradiation of white light (right). (B) PL decay curve of the NIR-CDs measured at the emission of 704 nm when excited at 480 nm.
PL emission spectra of the NIR-CDs at different pH values under excitation of 605 nm.
Cell viability of the NIR-CDs at increasing concentrations from 0 to 1 mg mL−1 on HeLa cells.

Confocal fluorescence micrographs of HeLa cells incubated with 30 μg mL−1 of the NIR-CDs for 0.5 h under bright field (A) and 578 nm excitation (B), respectively. In vivo PL image of a nude mouse treated with subcutaneous injection (C) and intravenous injection (D) of 50 μL of the NIR-CD aqueous solution. The color bars represent the fluorescence intensity.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent near-infrared emitting carbon dots derived from lemon juice and its bioimaging application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Safety Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Biology and Biotechnology, Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University, Shenzhen, 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Biology and Biotechnology
Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained intensive interests in the recent years due to their unique optical properties and widespread applications. However, the facile synthesis of highly efficient near-infrared emitting CDs (NIR-CDs) from renewable and low-cost natural resources is still a challenge to be addressed for their further applications, particularly in the bioimaging fields. Herein, NIR-CDs with a high quantum yield of 31% in water were synthesized by heating a formamide solution of pulp-free lemon juice. The as-obtained NIR-CDs possess an average size of 5.7 nm and a nitrogen content of approximately 20%, and exhibit an excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission at 704 nm. Further studies suggest that the intense near-infrared emission of NIR-CDs is originated from their surface states, and that the energy gaps of these surface states are modulated by both the particle size and the nitrogen-doping amount. In addition, characterized with good photostability and low cytotoxicity, these NIR-CDs can be used as an excellent fluorescent probe for bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new style of fluorescent nanomaterial, have gained intensive interests from scientists over the past decade [1–4]. Compared with the traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic fluorescent dyes, CDs possess numerous merits including unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, outstanding colloidal stability, low-cost, and easy surface functionalization [5–7]. These advantages endow them with great potential application in the field of bioimaging, light-emitting devices, biosensors, and more [8–10]. To date, scientists have made great efforts to prepare various CDs with high performances by the top-down methods and bottom-up strategies [3,11]. However, most of the reported CDs show intense emission merely in the blue-to-green light region with optimal excitation wavelength at the UV range, which strictly limited their further applications, especially in the bioimaging field, due to the strong tissue autofluorescence at short wavelength emissions and the severe photodamage caused by ultraviolet excitation [12–14]. In contrast, deep-red/near-infrared emissive CDs (wavelength beyond 650 nm) that can be excited by long-wavelength lights show a more promising feature in the biomedical field because of their increased tissue-penetration depths and decreased autofluorescence [15]. Therefore, it is important and urgent to produce deep-red or near-infrared emitting CDs with high quantum yields (QYs) under excitation of long-wavelength lights.

Up to now, there are just a few reports regarding the deep-red/near-infrared emitting CDs. For instance, Ge et al. produced deep-red emissive CDs with a PL peak at 680 nm by hydrothermally heating polythiophene derivatives [16]. Yang et al. prepared highly efficient near-infrared emissive CDs with a PL peak at 710 nm from hydrothermal treatment of dopamine and o-phenylenediamine [12]. Qu et al. synthesized near-infrared luminescent CDs with optimal emission at 760 nm through surface engineering of molecules or polymers [13]. Recently, our group also reported NIR-CDs with optimal peak at 715 nm by heating an aqueous solution of l-glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine [17]. Although impressive advances have been achieved in these pioneering works, there is still a great demand for developing new methods to produce near-infrared emissive CDs with high emission efficiency. Furthermore, their synthetic protocols usually require expensive or even toxic chemicals such as the benzene ring-containing compounds as the carbon sources, which is not benefit for low-cost and eco-friendly synthesis of NIR-CDs for widespread applications [18]. Hence, developing facile methods that use precursors directly from nature resources to produce NIR-CDs with superior optical properties and less environmental impact is highly desirable.

Lemon juice, as a kind of readily available, widely distributed, and renewable biomass, contains lots of hydrocarbon compounds necessary for CD formation. In this present research, we demonstrate a green, simple, and economical route to produce highly efficient NIR-CDs by solvothermally heating a formamide solution of lemon juice. The as-obtained NIR-CDs exhibit intense PL emission at 704 nm and a QY of 31%, which is among the top values ever recorded for NIR-CDs in water. After careful structural characterization and data analysis, the particle size and nitrogen-doping amount are believed to be determining factors synergistically responsible for the intense near-infrared emission of CDs. These NIR-CDs can be successfully employed as luminescent probes for in vitro/in vivo bioimaging due to their excellent water-solubility, stable PL, high QY and low toxicity.

Formamide, ethyl acetate, and methanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, High Glucose) were acquired from Gibco BRL (USA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and trypsinase were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were from commercial sources, and used without any further purification. The water used for all the experiments is ultrapure (Milli-Q) water.

A JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV was utilized for capturing high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of NIR-CDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on a PERSEE T10CS UV–Vis spectrometer with 1 cm path length quartz cuvettes. Fluorescence measurements were carried out on an F-4600 spectrofluorometer with excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm and 5 nm, respectively. FTIR analysis was performed on a VERTEX 80v spectrometer. The Raman spectra were obtained using a Senterra Raman spectrometer at a 785 nm excitation wavelength. The X-ray photoelectron spectra were acquired on a D8 Advance spectrometer. The photoluminescence decay was measured on an Edinburgh FS5 spectrometer.

In a typical procedure, 10 mL of pulp-free lemon juice was added to 30 mL of formamide. After stirring, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. Then, the resultant CD solution was purified via silica column chromatography using a mixture of ethyl acetate and methanol as the eluent. Finally, the obtained NIR-CDs were redispersed in water for subsequent characterization.

HeLa cells were plated at a density of 1 × 105 cells per mL in 96-well plates using Dulbecco's-modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10% FBS and incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37°Cfor 24 h. Afterward, the culture medium was replaced with 200 mL of DMEM containing the NIR-CDs at a wide concentration range from 50 to 1000 μg mL−1. About 24 h after incubation, 20 mL of 5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added into each well. After incubation for another 4 h, the culture medium was carefully removed, and 150 mL of DMSO was then added to dissolve the MTT. Finally, the mixture was shaken for 15 min at room temperature, and the absorbance of each well at 492 nm was measured using an automatic ELISA analyzer (SPR-960). Control experiments without the NIR-CDs were also performed. Each experiment was conducted five times, and the averaged data were presented.

In brief, 1.0 mL of HeLa cells in a Dulbecco's-modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were seeded into 6-well culture plates at a density of 4 × 104 cells per mL, and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. After the DMEM was removed, a mixture of NIR-CDs (30 μg mL−1) in DMEM was added to each well for another 0.5 h of incubation. Subsequently, the cells were washed in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) three times to remove the non-specifically bound NIR-CDs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. Finally, cellular fluorescence images were captured by an FV10i laser scanning confocal microscope.

The animal experiments were approved by the China Committee for Research and Animal Ethics in compliance with the guidelines and regulations from the institutional animal care and use committee of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. A 6-week-old nude mouse (balb/c) was subcutaneously injected with 50 μL of an aqueous solution of the NIR-CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) at the injection site after being anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital. Then, the animal fluorescence imaging was collected by using a Bruker in vivo imaging apparatus under an excitation light of 588 nm and an emission filter of 700 nm. The exposure time was 1.0 s for all fluorescence images. Furthermore, the observation of NIR-CD circulation in the whole-body was performed by the tail intravenous injection of NIR-CD aqueous solution into mouse under the same experimental procedures. All animal experiments were conducted at the Laboratory Animal Centre of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

The quantum yield (QY) of the NIR-CDs was measured using an integrating sphere attached to an FS5 spectrofluorometer. First, the NIR-CD aqueous solution was diluted to an absorption intensity of below 0.1 at the best excitation wavelength (605 nm). Then, this aqueous solution was added into a 10 mm fluorescence cuvette, placed in the integrating sphere and excited with monochromatic light of 605 nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the ranges of 595–615 and 485–900 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the same fluorescence spectra for pure water were also measured under the same conditions. Finally, the QYs were calculated using fluorescence software based on the PL spectrum of both the sample and the water. Each experiment was conducted three times in parallel, and the average value of QY was presented.

Near-infrared emissive CDs were obtained through solvothermal treatment of pulp-free lemon juice in formamide at 200 °C for 6 h, followed by purification via silica column chromatography. The as-obtained NIR-CDs solution was concentrated by rotary evaporator, freeze-dried, and then redispersed in water for further characterization. In Fig. 1 A, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the NIR-CDs displays that these nanoparticles are uniform and well mono-dispersed in water, with an average particle size of approximately 5.7 nm (Fig. S1) [19]. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image provided in the inset shows a well-resolved crystal lattice with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, representing the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes [20]. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of the NIR-CDs in Fig. 1B, reveals their topographic heights to be 2.5 nm, corresponding to 5 to 6 layers of graphene-like sheets [21]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the NIR-CDs, as shown in Fig. S2, has an obvious peak at around 23°, which is ascribed to an interlayer spacing of 0.32 nm. The Raman spectrum of these NIR-CDs in Fig. 2 A exhibits a D band at 1349 cm−1 and a G band at 1591 cm−1, corresponding to areas of disordered structures and graphitic structures, respectively, of carbon materials [22]. The intensity ratio ID/IG is 0.76, much lower than that of many other reported CDs [23–25], which indicates that these NIR-CDs are highly graphitized. This conclusion is consistent with the TEM and AFM results.

To further determine the elemental composition and chemical structures of the NIR-CDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out for these samples. In Fig. 2B, the FT-IR spectrum reveals that the NIR-CDs have abundant hydrophilic groups on their surfaces, such as OH at 3414 cm−1, NH at 3157 cm−1, CO at 1651 cm−1, and OH at 1266 cm−1 [26]. These hydrophilic groups ensure their excellent solubility in water. Moreover, stretching vibrations of CN bond at 1600 cm−1, CC bond at 1539 cm−1, and CN bond at 1447 cm−1 were found for these NIR-CDs, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures in the CD cores during the reaction process [27]. The wide XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 3 A exhibits three typical peaks of C1s (284eV), N1s (400eV), and O1s (531 eV), suggesting that the NIR-CD samples are mainly comprised of C, N and O elements. It is worth noting that the nitrogen-doping amount is as high as 20% in our NIR-CD samples, much higher than that of many other reported red luminescent CDs [28–30], indicating that a large amount of nitrogen atoms can be incorporated into CDs through the dehydration reactions. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C 1s band (Fig. 3B) can be deconvoluted into four peaks: CC/CC (284.3 eV), CN (285.8 eV), CO (286.5 eV), and CO/CN (288.1 eV). The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.1, 399.4, and 400.5 eV, which could be attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [31]. The O 1s band in Fig. 3D contains two peaks at 531.5 and 533.2 eV for CO and CO, respectively [32]. The above data, together with the TEM, FTIR, and Raman results, clearly demonstrate that the NIR-CDs consisted of large π-conjugated domains in their cores and amorphous regions on their surfaces, and that the NIR-CDs samples are heavily doped with nitrogen atoms.

The optical properties of the NIR-CDs were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the UV–visible absorption spectrum displays significant absorption in both the UV and the visible regions. In the UV region, two obvious absorption peaks were found at 237 and 279 nm respectively, corresponding to the π−π* transitions of the CC and CN bonds in aromatic rings, which usually do not generate long-wavelength fluorescence [33]. In the visible region, the NIR-CDs exhibit a noticeable absorption band at 611 nm, attributed to the n-π* transition of surface states containing many CN/CN and CO/CO structures [29]. Such a characteristic absorption peak imparts intense long-wavelength emission to the NIR-CDs. Similar with previously reported CD fractions [9], these separated NIR-CDs also display an excitation-independent PL emission, with the emission peak around 704 nm (Fig. 4A). Upon the excitation of white light, the NIR-CD aqueous solution can immediately emit bright and stable luminescence in deep-red color (Fig. 4A inset). The absolute QY of the NIR-CDs is determined to be 31% by an integrating sphere under their optimal excitation wavelength of 605 nm, which is remarkable for near-infrared emissive CDs at present [5]. The time-resolved PL decay curve of the NIR-CD samples was also measured by the time-correlated single photon counting method. The results in Fig. 4B indicate that the decay curve is perfectly fitted by a dual-exponential formula with a mean lifetime of 1.57 ns, which is comparable with those of the most reported CDs [17,34], indicating that a strong coupling exists between the surface states and the intrinsic states [35]. These results clearly disclose that the intense NIR emissions of the NIR-CDs are related to their surface states, which is totally different from those previously reported blue emissive CDs synthesized from hydrothermal treatment of an solution of pulp-free lemon juice [14,36].

It has been widely accepted that quantum size effects, different elements doping, carbon core states, and surface states are the dominant factors controlling the fluorescence emissions of CDs [37]. In our previous reports, PL tunable CDs from blue to red were obtained by heating treatment of a solution of pulp-free lemon juice dissolved in different amounts of ethanol, and the nitrogen- and oxygen-related surface states on CD surfaces together with nitrogen-derived structures in carbon cores were proven to synergistically contribute to their intense PL emission [14]. However, in the present research, solvothermally heating the same carbon source lemon juice in formamide yielded NIR fluorescent CDs with high emission efficiency, suggesting that reaction solvent formamide is very crucial for the production of NIR-CDs. To further explore the main PL origin of the NIR-CDs, CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3 were specifically synthesized just by decreasing the dosage of formamide from 30 to 15, 10, and to 5 mL, respectively. Then, these as-obtained CD samples were purified and analyzed to investigate the influence of dosage of formamide on the microstructure and emission characteristics of CDs. In Fig. S3 and Fig. 4, the TEM images and XPS results reveal that, as the formamide content decreases, the three CDs exhibit an obvious decrease of particle size from 4.8 to 2.1 nm, and also a decrease of nitrogen content from 17 to 5%, indicating that the reaction solvent formamide had a significant influence on the CD formation through controlling the dehydration reaction and carbonization degree [17]. Moreover, the PL spectra of the three CDs show an evident PL blue shift from 648 to 469 nm with the decrease of the formamide content, as shown in Fig. S5. These results clearly demonstrate that the particle size and nitrogen-doping amount can be controlled by adjusting the dosage of formamide, further regulating the optical properties of NIR-CDs. It has been reported that the surface states of CDs is the synergistic hybridization of the carbon backbones and the connected functional groups, and their energy gap is related to the extent of the π-electron conjugation and the surface chemistry [11,38,39]. The larger π-electron conjugation system or the higher nitrogen-doping amount will make the energy gap of surface states narrower, so as to make the PL emission shift to the long-wavelength region [30,40]. As a result, near-infrared emitting CDs can be obtained by increasing both the particle size and the nitrogen-doping amount. In addition, to further confirm that the intense near-infrared luminescence is based on their surface states, the pH-dependent PL behaviors of the NIR-CDs were also investigated. The results in Fig. 5 show that the PL intensity of the NIR-CDs reach the maximum under neutral environments, and the PL peak red shifts as the pH value decreases, which are in accordance with the surface-state controlled PL behaviors reported in other literature [9,41]. Based on the above data and reported literature, we believe that the PL emission of the NIR-CDs is originated from their surface-states, and that the energy gaps of these surface states heavily depend on the particle size, as well as the nitrogen-doping amount.

Considering the superior optical properties of the NIR-CDs in water, their potential application in bioimaging was attempted. Before biological application, the photostability of the NIR-CD aqueous solution was tested at first. The results in Fig. S6 demonstrate that the NIR-CDs are quite stable with only slight PL alterations even after continuous UV irradiation for 2 h [42]. Then, the cytotoxicity of the NIR-CDs toward HeLa cells was evaluated using the standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays. As shown in Fig. 6 , over 90% of cell viabilities were observed for HeLa cells after 48 h of incubation with the NIR-CDs even at high concentrations up to 1 mg mL−1, revealing the very low cytotoxicity of this material [43]. Thus, the NIR-CDs could be safely applied for in vitro cell imaging. In Fig. 7 A and B, after an incubation of 0.5 h with the NIR-CDs (30 μg mL−1), the HeLa cells became bright and retained good morphologies when observed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM), indicating that the NIR-CDs are capable of passing through the cell membranes and get into the cells [44–46]. After that, the in vivo mouse imaging capability of the NIR-CDs was also tested using the NIR-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 7C, after subcutaneous injection of 50 μL of the NIR-CD aqueous solution, a much higher PL intensity than the background signal produced by the mouse skin can be observed at the injection site, indicating that the intense near-infrared luminescence of NIR-CDs can penetrate mouse skin and tissues efficiently. Furthermore, the same dosage of NIR-CD aqueous solution was intravenously injected into a nude mouse through the tail vein, and after 15 min of whole-body circulation, bright fluorescence was observed in the bladder (Fig. 7D), implying that the intravenously injected NIR-CDs are primarily excreted via urine. All these points unambiguously demonstrate that the NIR-CDs produced from lemon juice can serve as a nontoxic and a near-infrared luminescent probe for bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo.

In summary, we have developed a one-step, economical and environmentally friendly method to produce highly efficient near-infrared luminescent CDs from lemon juice. The as-prepared NIR-CDs are nanometer-sized, emit bright and stable excitation-independent photoluminescence in water, and have outstanding photostability and minimal cytotoxicity. These outstanding characteristics ensure the successful application of the NIR-CDs in bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo. In addition, the strong near-infrared luminescence of the NIR-CDs was demonstrated to have originated from their surface states whose energy gaps are modulated by both the particle size and the nitrogen-doping amount. This work provides a simple and inexpensive route toward production of high-quality near infrared emissive CDs from biomass resources in an environment friendly manner. More importantly, these NIR-CDs hold promise for the fields of sensing, bioimaging, light emitting devices, and other related applications.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017QNA08).
